turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court in case 21-1052, United States, ex rel. Polansky v. Executive Health Resources.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: The False Claims Act imposes civil liability on those who defraud the Federal Government. So as you might expect, federal prosecutors can sue alleged violators. But this is an unusual statute because it also allows private parties to sue on the government's behalf. In the odd terminology of this statute, those people are called relators and the case they bring on behalf on the government is called a qui tam action. If a qui tam action is successful, the relator gets a cut of the recovery but the suit is brought in the name of the government and the injury asserted is the governments. So the government remands what lawyers call the real party in interest. Because relators are unusual plaintiffs, they are subject to unusual restrictions. Right from the start, the relator has to file his complaint under seal and serve a copy on the government alone not on the opposing party. The government then gets a window, which is called the seal period in which to decide whether to intervene in the case. If the government chooses to intervene, it takes over the suit. If it declines, the relator gets to conduct the action but even then, the relator isn't home free because the statute gives the government certain continuing rights in the litigation. This case concerns the scope of those rights. The main question is whether the government has a right to dismiss a qui tam suit when it did not intervene during the seal period. The government says it can always get a qui tam suits dismissed even if it hasn't intervened. The relator says the opposite that if the government passes on its chance to intervene in the seal period, it can't get dismissal later. In our view, neither side has it right. The text of the statute is complicated but when we think that when it's parsed carefully, the answer it gives is some place in between the parties' positions. On the one hand, the government has to have intervened in the suit before it can seek to dismiss it. The government can't get dismissal as a non-party but the intervention does not need to have happened in the seal period. The statute allows the government to intervene later on in the suit upon showing of good cause. And if the government does that, it takes back the right to control the suit including the right to dismiss it. So dismissal depends on intervention but intervention can happen at any time. That view also fits the statute's purposes. The government's interest in the qui tam suit is the predominant one both before and after the seal period ends. And after the seal period, new information may come to light effecting the government's view of the suit and whether it should go forward. So Congress gave the government a continuing right to assess the suit, to intervene later and to get the suit dismissed on a reasonable showing that dismissal best serves the government's interests. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion, in which Justice Barrett joins. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion.
